questions.text: Who was the last of the powerful Mughal emperors?
questions.explanation: Aurangzeb was the last powerful Mughal ruler; after his death in 1707, the empire began to fragment.

questions.text: In 1600, the East India Company acquired a charter from which ruler?
questions.explanation: The charter granted the Company the sole right to trade with the East.

questions.text: Which European power was the first to establish a base in India and where?
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese explorer, discovered the sea route to India in 1498.

questions.text: What was the main item of trade that European companies were interested in from India?
options: Cotton, silk, and spices like pepper
questions.explanation: Fine qualities of cotton and silk produced in India had a big market in Europe.

questions.text: The first English factory was set up on the banks of the river ________ in 1651.
questions.explanation: This Hugli factory became the base from which the Company’s traders, known at that time as 'factors', operated.

questions.text: The Company persuaded Aurangzeb to issue a 'farman' granting it the right to:
options: Collect taxes from all over India
questions.explanation: A farman is a royal edict or order. The Company used this to avoid paying customs duties.

questions.text: Who was the Nawab of Bengal during the Battle of Plassey?
questions.explanation: Sirajuddaulah was the Nawab who challenged the Company's growing power in Bengal.

questions.text: In which year did the Battle of Plassey take place?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey is famous because it was the first major victory the Company won in India.

questions.text: One of the main reasons for the defeat of Sirajuddaulah at Plassey was the treachery of:
questions.explanation: Mir Jafar, one of the Nawab’s commanders, did not fight the battle as he was promised the throne by the British.

questions.text: Which battle was fought in 1764 between the British and the combined forces of Mir Qasim, Shujauddaulah, and Shah Alam II?
questions.explanation: The victory at Buxar confirmed the British as masters of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa.

questions.text: In 1765, the Mughal Emperor appointed the East India Company as the ________ of the province of Bengal.
questions.explanation: The Diwani allowed the Company to use the vast revenue resources of Bengal.

questions.text: What was the 'Subsidiary Alliance' system?
options: A system to help Indian kings fight each other
A treaty where Indian rulers were forced to keep British troops and pay for them
An alliance between the French and the British
questions.explanation: If the Indian rulers failed to make the payment, part of their territory was taken away as penalty.

questions.text: Which Governor-General introduced the Subsidiary Alliance?
questions.explanation: Under Wellesley, states like Hyderabad and Awadh were forced to cede territory.

questions.text: Tipu Sultan was the ruler of:
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan, known as the 'Tiger of Mysore', was a formidable enemy of the British.

questions.text: Tipu Sultan died defending his capital ________ in 1799.
questions.explanation: He was defeated in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War.

questions.text: The Maratha power was finally crushed in the:
questions.explanation: The Peshwa was removed and sent to Bithur near Kanpur with a pension.

questions.text: The 'Doctrine of Lapse' was a policy devised by:
questions.explanation: It stated that if an Indian ruler died without a male heir, his kingdom would 'lapse' and become part of Company territory.

questions.text: Which of these states was NOT annexed under the Doctrine of Lapse?
questions.explanation: Mysore was conquered through war, not the Doctrine of Lapse.

questions.text: Awadh was annexed by the British in 1856 on the grounds of:
questions.explanation: The British argued they were 'obliged by duty' to free the people from the 'misgovernment' of the Nawab.

questions.text: What does the term 'Nabobs' refer to?
options: British Company servants who returned to Britain with great wealth
questions.explanation: It was an anglicized version of the Indian word 'Nawab'.

questions.text: Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General, divided the British territories into administrative units called:
questions.explanation: There were three Presidencies: Bengal, Madras and Bombay.

questions.text: Under the new system of justice (1772), each district was to have two courts: a criminal court and a ________ court.
questions.explanation: The criminal court was called Faujdari Adalat and the civil court was Diwani Adalat.

questions.text: Who was the 'Collector' in the British administrative structure?
options: A person who collected taxes only
The main figure in an Indian district responsible for law, order, and revenue
questions.explanation: The Collectorate became the new center of power and patronage.

questions.text: The British used 'Paramountcy' as a policy to claim that:
options: They were equal to Indian kings
Their authority was supreme and they could annex any Indian state
questions.explanation: This policy was initiated under Governor-General Lord Hastings.

questions.text: Who led an anti-British resistance movement in Kitoor (Karnataka)?
questions.explanation: She was arrested in 1824 and died in prison in 1829.

questions.text: The East India Company transformed from a trading company to a ________ colonial power.
questions.explanation: New technologies like steamships helped them tighten their grip on India.

questions.text: Before steamships, how long did it take to travel to India from Britain by sea?
questions.explanation: Steamships reduced the travel time to three weeks.

questions.text: Who was the Mughal Emperor when the Company became the Diwan of Bengal?
questions.explanation: He granted the Diwani after the defeat in the Battle of Buxar.

questions.text: The company’s army was mainly composed of Indian soldiers known as:
questions.explanation: Derived from the Hindi word 'sipahi', meaning soldier.

questions.text: Lord Hastings initiated a new policy of 'Paramountcy'. Which state resisted this and saw its queen lead a revolt?
questions.explanation: Rani Chennamma of Kitoor fought against the annexation.

questions.text: In 1773, which act was passed to regulate the East India Company?
questions.explanation: The Regulating Act of 1773 established the Governor-General of Bengal.

questions.text: Which of these European countries did NOT have a trading company in India?
questions.explanation: Dutch, Danish, French, and British all had significant trading interests.

questions.text: What happened during the 'Great Famine of 1770' in Bengal?
questions.explanation: The famine was aggravated by the Company's high revenue demands.

questions.text: Who was the 'Tiger of Mysore'?
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan is remembered for his bravery and resistance to British rule.

questions.text: The Company's monopoly on trade was granted by:
options: A charter from Queen Elizabeth I
The Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II
questions.explanation: The charter prevented other English trading groups from competing with the Company in the East.

questions.text: Which territory was taken by the Company in 1849 after two bloody wars?
questions.explanation: The Punjab was annexed after the death of Maharaja Ranjit Singh.

questions.text: Muskets and Matchlocks were:
options: Early forms of firearms used by soldiers
questions.explanation: As technology changed, the infantry requirements of the Company’s army became more important.

questions.text: The British introduced a 'uniform military culture'. What was its main feature?
questions.explanation: Soldiers were increasingly subjected to European-style training and discipline.

questions.text: What was the result of the 'Diwani' for the Company?
options: It could now use Indian revenues to buy Indian goods and maintain troops
questions.explanation: It solved the problem of the Company needing to import gold and silver from Britain to buy goods.

questions.text: By 1857, the Company had direct rule over about ________ per cent of the territory of the Indian subcontinent.
questions.explanation: With indirect influence over the remaining territory, the Company virtually had all of India under its control.
